title: Hello! My name is Fangzhou (Eric) Liu!
text:
Instagram
  
  
     ORCID
  
  
    
     Github
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  




Hello! My name is Fangzhou (Eric) Liu!
I am a full-time biostatistician in the Department of Population and Quantitative Health Sciences at Case Western Reserve University, as well as part of the Thoracic Oncology Program at University Hospitals. Meanwhile, I am also a part-time first-year Clinical Translational Science PhD student.
My work spans cancer epidemiology, health outcomes research, spatial analysis, and applied machine learning. I specialize in R, Python, Tableau, and ArcGIS Pro, and I am passionate about building rigorous, reproducible analyses that support scientific discovery and real-world decision-making.
I am involved in several projects that integrate clinical data and social determinants of health. My current research includes cancer outcomes analysis using large administrative datasets, time-to-event and survival modeling, and spatial studies examining health disparities across communities.
Outside of research, I enjoy photography, travel, and spending time with my wife, Yuan, and our cat, Kaba.
Welcome to my website, and thank you for visiting!
Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions or would like to discuss potential collaborations.

title: Blog
section: What I Learned About Being a Modern Scientist
text:
What I Learned About Being a Modern Scientist
March 8th 2026
One of the most meaningful lessons I learned from reading How to Be a Modern Scientist by Jeffrey Leek is that modern scientific work is no longer limited to publishing papers. Traditionally, scientists were evaluated mostly based on peer-reviewed journal articles, but the landscape of science is rapidly changing. Today, communication, data sharing, reproducible code, and open collaboration are becoming essential parts of the scientific process. This idea changed how I think about what it means to contribute to science.
A concept that stood out to me was the importance of openness and reproducibility. Modern scientists are encouraged to share their data, code, and research materials so that others can verify and build upon their work. According to Leek, sharing data and code is a cornerstone of modern scientific practice because it allows results to be checked and reused by other researchers. This approach improves transparency and strengthens the credibility of scientific findings. As someone who works with statistical analysis and programming, I realized that making analyses reproducible is just as important as producing the results themselves.
Another idea I found valuable is the role of communication outside traditional journals. Modern scientists can use tools like blogs, preprints, and social media to share their work quickly and engage with the broader community. These tools allow research to reach audiences much faster than the traditional publishing process, which can take months or even years. They also provide opportunities for feedback and collaboration from researchers across the world.
Finally, this reading helped me see that being a modern scientist requires more than technical expertise. It also involves sharing knowledge openly, communicating clearly, and contributing to a broader scientific community. In many ways, scientists today function not only as researchers but also as educators, programmers, and communicators. Adopting these practices can increase the impact of research and help science progress more efficiently.
Overall, the key lesson I learned is that modern science values openness, collaboration, and transparency. By sharing data, publishing reproducible analyses, and communicating research widely, scientists can make their work more useful and impactful. This perspective has encouraged me to think more carefully about how I conduct and present my own research in the future.
Reference: Leek, Jeffrey. How to be a Modern Scientist. Leanpub, 2016.

title: Photography Collection: Life With Kaba
text:
This gallery features eight of my favorite photos, all centered around our cat Kaba, who is truly the heart of our home. Four of the photos capture special moments between my wife and Kaba, showing their bond and everyday happiness.
The final two photos include a bit of Kaba’s mischievous personality:

One shows my wife with Kaba, sitting next to the book he bit.
The other is me with Kaba, with the same “victim” book (different places though) in the background.

Together, these images tell a small story about our little family — the love, the playfulness, and the funny moments Kaba brings into our lives.
